coredata.dc:title: Laccase-mimicking Cu@CDs-enabled bioinspired immunosensor using CuO nanoparticles for voltametric detection of serum thyroxine level in patients with hyperthyroidism or hypothyroidism
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Farzin, Mohammad Ali
Naghib, Seyed Morteza
coredata.dc:description: In this study, for the first time, a voltammetric bioinspired immunosensor was fabricated to determine free thyroxine (T4) based on the blocking nanozyme-dependent signaling pathway through masking the active sites of the laccase-mimicking Cu-doped CDs (Cu@CDs). The focus shifted to monitoring a vital biomarker for thyroid diseases using a metal-containing nanozyme, showcasing high potential to catalyze the oxidation of phenolic compounds. Thanks to the active sites of Cu@CD nanozyme, the electrocatalytic oxidation of hydroquinone (HQ), as a signal probe, was performed at a high rate. The CuO nanostructures also increased the conductivity as well as specific surface area of electrode. Despite signal amplification of HQ, the binding of antibody to carbon dot reduced enzymatic activity of nanozyme and subsequently resulted in a considerable decrease in the current intensity of GCE/CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs-HQ. The bioconjugation of T4 with anti-T4 antibody resulted in the further coverage of the active sites of nanozyme. Therefore, the current of GCE/CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs-HQ/Ab decreased proportionally to the T4 concentration in a DLR (dynamic linear range) of 10.0 pM-8.0 nM which comprises the reference range for free T4 (11.5–22.7 pM). The LOD (limit of detection) of 2.8 pM, was lower compared to the values reported in previous studies. Furthermore, this study exhibited the key finding that the highly specific interaction of T4 with immobilized antibody led to a remarkable diminish in the immunosensor signal compared to the interferers. More importantly, spike-recovery studies demonstrated the high potential of the designed immunosensor to detect serum T4 levels in patients with hyperthyroidism or hypothyroidism.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fabrication procedure of CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs-HQ/Ab-based electrochemical sensing device for T4 detection.
(A) SEM image (50 KX magnification), EDX spectrum (inset of Fig. 2A) and (B) XRD spectrum of CuO nanoparticles.

XPS survey spectrum (A) and XPS high-resolution survey of Cu 2p (inset of Fig. 3A) and C 1s, N 1s, O 1s (bottom of Fig. 3A) of Cu-doped CDs; TEM image (B), DLS spectrum (inset of Fig. 3B), FTIR spectrum (C) of Cu-doped CDs; FL spectra (D) of Cu-doped CDs (curve a), undoped CDs (curve b) and blue fluorescence of Cu-doped CD solution under UV lamp (inset of Fig. 3D).

(A) CV voltammograms of GCE/CuO nanoparticles/HQ (curve a), GCE/Cu@CDs/HQ (curve b), GCE/CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs/HQ (0.05 mM) (curve c), GCE/CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs/HQ/Anti-T4 Ab (10 µM) (curve d) and GCE/CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs/HQ/Anti-T4 Ab/T4 (40 pM) (curve e) in 0.1 M PBS (pH=7.2) and scan rate of 100 mV s −1; (B) The masking procedure of the active sites of the nanozyme by antibodies that leads to the blocking of electron transfer between HQ and Cu@CDs.
Optimization of (A) HQ concentration, (B) Ab concentration and (C) incubation time of antibody-target interaction at room temperature in the 0.1 M PBS (pH=7.2).

(A) The DPV voltammograms of the GCE/CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs-HQ/Ab at different concentrations of T4 from 0.0 to 8.0 nM in 0.1 M PBS (pH=7.2); (B) Response changes versus variations of T4 concentration (calibration plot) in a linear range of 10.0–8.0 pM in 0.1 M PBS (pH=7.2); (C) Calibration plot obtained with different concentrations of T4 (clinical); (D) Selectivity of GCE/CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs-HQ/Ab at the presence of interferes such as TSH (1 nM), BSA (1 nM), PTH (1 nM), hemoglobin (1 nM) and their mixture in 0.1 M PBS (pH=7.2); (E) Long-term stability of GCE/CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs-HQ/Ab after its storage in 0.1 M PBS (pH=7.2) at 4 °C for 30 days.
Comparison of the designed immunosensor with other electrochemical sensors for T4 determination.

head.ce:title: Laccase-mimicking Cu@CDs-enabled bioinspired immunosensor using CuO nanoparticles for voltametric detection of serum thyroxine level in patients with hyperthyroidism or hypothyroidism

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology Department, School of Advanced Technologies, Iran University of Science and Technology (IUST), Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Iran University of Science and Technology (IUST)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanotechnology Department, School of Advanced Technologies, Iran University of Science and Technology (IUST), Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, for the first time, a voltammetric bioinspired immunosensor was fabricated to determine free thyroxine (T4) based on the blocking nanozyme-dependent signaling pathway through masking the active sites of the laccase-mimicking Cu-doped CDs (Cu@CDs). The focus shifted to monitoring a vital biomarker for thyroid diseases using a metal-containing nanozyme, showcasing high potential to catalyze the oxidation of phenolic compounds. Thanks to the active sites of Cu@CD nanozyme, the electrocatalytic oxidation of hydroquinone (HQ), as a signal probe, was performed at a high rate. The CuO nanostructures also increased the conductivity as well as specific surface area of electrode. Despite signal amplification of HQ, the binding of antibody to carbon dot reduced enzymatic activity of nanozyme and subsequently resulted in a considerable decrease in the current intensity of GCE/CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs-HQ. The bioconjugation of T4 with anti-T4 antibody resulted in the further coverage of the active sites of nanozyme. Therefore, the current of GCE/CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs-HQ/Ab decreased proportionally to the T4 concentration in a DLR (dynamic linear range) of 10.0 pM-8.0 nM which comprises the reference range for free T4 (11.5–22.7 pM). The LOD (limit of detection) of 2.8 pM, was lower compared to the values reported in previous studies. Furthermore, this study exhibited the key finding that the highly specific interaction of T4 with immobilized antibody led to a remarkable diminish in the immunosensor signal compared to the interferers. More importantly, spike-recovery studies demonstrated the high potential of the designed immunosensor to detect serum T4 levels in patients with hyperthyroidism or hypothyroidism.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: There is no doubt that the involvement of biosensors in our daily life has changed our lifestyle. The realization of smart homes, smart cities and etc through the integration of advanced biosensors with the Internet of Things (IoTs) technology has led to the ease of our lives [1]. On the other hand, even though biosensors hold a critical place in our lives, biosensor technology has various limitations in performance, biocompatibility, stability and cost. Biomimicry or bioinspiration, which involves mimicking the natural structures, biological mechanisms, and processes of living organisms in the man-made systems, appears to be a potential remedy to overcome such challenges related to biosensors. In the simplest terms, biosensors inspired by biological systems combine nature-inspired principles and sensing strategies to design more efficient and stable biosensors, which can overcome most of the inherent limitations associated with conventional sensing devices [2].

The development of engineered nanoparticles with the aim of mimicking natural structures and activities, as well as their integration with biosensors, has been very successful in designing high-performance sensing devices. Especially, nanoparticles with enzyme-like activities, termed as nanozymes, show great potential for use in the biocatalyst-based sensors due to their inherent benefits of low cost, high stability, good durability, tunable activity, convenient synthesis and modification [3]. Nanozymes, as the nanomaterial-based synthetic enzymes, mimic catalytic sites of natural enzymes. Compared to natural enzymes, they exhibit more catalytic activity and tolerance to the surrounding environment, such as temperature and pH [3]. In addition, nanozyme-based biomimetic cascade catalysis can confer very high sensitivity to biosensors [4]. More importantly, nanozymes have great potential for the personalized diagnosis [5]. These advantages make nanozyme-based biosensors promising tools for realizing their transition from the laboratory to the market.

In recent years, carbon dot (CD)-based nanozymes including laccase-like [6], oxidase-like [7], peroxidase-like [8] and superoxide dismutase-like [9] have attracted increasing research interest due to their great capability for the biological applications. These carbon-based quantum dots exhibit outstanding advantages such as prominent light harvesting, low cost, tunable fluorescence with high quantum yield, substantial specific surface area, good biocompatibility, ultralow toxicity, facile synthesis and modification [10,11]. In addition, they have a good electron transfer, indicating their high potential as catalysts [12,13]. The heteroatom dopants such as Cu boost electron transfer inside the CDs [14–16]. Cu-doped CDs (Cu@CDs) present an excellent electron transfer capacity and a great stability [17]. On the other hand, laccase-like catalytic activity of CDs is promoted by the Cu doping [17]. The carbon dots act as the skeleton and the active coppers act as the active center, just like the active center of laccase. Laccase is known as a Cu-containing oxidoreductase enzyme that can catalyze the oxidation of chemicals with ortho and para diphenol groups [17,18]. Although laccase has good catalytic oxidation ability, it suffers from poor stability [19]. To enhance the activity and stability of laccase, nanozymes such as Cu@CD can be utilized. One of the important advantages of laccase-like nanozymes over copper-containing complexes is their high ability to catalyze the one-electron oxidation of the substrates without the need for hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) [20,21]. Similar to laccase, oxidation of substrate in presence of nanozymes is performed without the use of H2O2.

The main objective of this work is to fabricate a bioinspired voltammetric immunosensor using the laccase-mimicking Cu@CDs and copper oxide (CuO) nanoparticles for signal-off detection of free thyroxine (T4) in the real sample medium. To our knowledge, this is the first study to use a carbon dot-based nanozyme for signal amplification of electrochemical immunosensor. Although, considerable efforts have been made to couple nanozyme-based catalysis and redox cycling for signal enhancement of electrochemical immunosensors [22,23], signal amplification induced by carbon dot-based nanozyme with laccase-like activity has not been reported so far.

The CuO has been widely utilized in the designing electrochemical sensing devices owing to the distinct properties such as high natural abundance, low-cost production, high conductivity, good electrochemical properties [24–27]. The CuO nanostructures provide a high surface area for the electrochemical activities with enhanced sensing properties [28–30]. Here, thyroxine, the main hormone secreted by the thyroid gland in the bloodstream, was selected as a model. This iodine-bonded derivative of the amino acid tyrosine (tyrosine-based hormone), which is mainly produced in the epithelial thyroid cells, controls the body's metabolism. The designed nanozyme-based immunosensor was able to monitor lower- or higher-than-normal T4 levels in the hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism diseases, respectively.

Copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4, ≥98.0 %), copper(II) nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2, 99.0–104 %), Polyvinyl olyvidone (olyvidone, wt 40,000), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, ≥98.0 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥98 %), hydrazine hydrate solution (80 wt %), Triton X-100 (t-Octylphenoxypolyethoxyethanol, ∼10 % in H2O), hydroquinone (HQ, ≥98 %), bovine serum albumin (BSA, ≥98.0 % lyophilized powder), hemoglobin, parathyroid hormone (Fragment 1–34 human, ≥95.0 %), thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH, ≥95 %), L-thyroxine solution (T4, ≥98 %), potassium hexacyanoferrate(III) (K3Fe(CN)6, 99.98 %) and N-Ethyl-N′-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, ≥98.0 %) were prepared from Sigma-Aldrich company (USA). Phosphate buffer aqueous solution (PBS, pH=7.2) was purchased from Notron Company (Iran).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal synthesis of laccase-mimicking Cu-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Eco-friendly one-pot synthesis of Cu-doped CDs was carried out by green hydrothermal method from biomass using an herb as a carbon source. Hydrothermal synthesis was selected because of its many advantages such as high product purity, low-cost, ability to control the nanoparticle size, mass efficiency and simplicity [31]. Here, 0.5 g salix aegyptiaca (Persian willow) leaves powder (prepared from Kashan, Iran) as the precursor, 0.1 g Cu(NO3)2 and 50 mL double-distilled water entered into a 100 mL Teflon chamber of high-quality stainless-steel hydrothermal reactor and heated for 9 h in an oven pre-heated to 180 °C. The hydrothermal reactor was allowed to cool to room temperature. The synthesized light brown solution of carbon dot was then centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 40 min. Finally, the Cu@CDs aqueous solution was dialyzed against deionized water through a dialysis membrane used in form of a dialysis bag.

To produce CuO nanoparticles, a simple method was followed according to the previous publication [27]. At first, 1 g of CuSO4·5H2O was added to 200 mL of double-distilled water. Then, 500 mg of polyvidone (a water-soluble polymer with good biostability) was slowly added while the solution was stirred vigorously. A blue suspended solution was formed by drop-wise addition of 40 ml of 0.2 M NaOH. Finally, a color change from blue to brick red was observed by adding 150 µL of hydrazine hydrate as a strong reducing agent and subsequently stirring for 20 min at the room temperature. The hydrazine hydrate is used to reduce metal cations especially copper and silver to metal nanoparticles [32]. The brownish red colloid was centrifuged and the obtaining sample was dried overnight at 60 °C after washing several times.

Ex-situ electrodeposition of nanostructured coating was performed through immersing the GCE in the buffer (BBS, 0.1 M, pH= 7.2) containing 0.15 mg mL-1 of CuO nanoparticles. Then, a potential range of -1.4 to 0.7 V against Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode was applied by CV with a scan rate of 0.1 V s-1 for 15 consecutive cycles [27]. After electrodeposition, the modified electrode was washed with double-distilled water to release undeposited nanoparticles. For further modification, the laccase-mimicking Cu@CDs aqueous solution after dialysis was pipetted on the GCE/CuO nanoparticles to cover the entire electrode surface. The GCE/CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs was then eluted with double-distilled water and dried at room temperature. After immobilization of Cu@CD nanozyme, the electrochemical signaling pathway was engineered by pipetting 0.05 mM HQ (an important substrate of laccases) on the electrode surface through the formation of HQ-CD complex via noncovalent interactions [33]. On the other hand, HQ was adsorbed on the surface of CDs containing hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups owing to the hydrogen bonding (dipole–dipole attraction) [33]. More importantly, laccase-mimicking Cu@CDs exhibited high catalytic capability for the oxidation of HQ to quinone without using H2O2 [6]. In fact, the H2O2-free oxidation of HQ is closely associated with the excellent catalytic activity of Cu@CDs because these quantum dots can accelerate the electron-transfer process. To design recognition strategy, aminated T4-specific antibodies (NH2-Ab) were immobilized on the modified electrode surface in a random fashion through cross-linking between the abundant pendant primary amine bearing Abs and CDs containing activated carboxylic groups (-COOH). Since the primary amine groups are widely distributed on the Ab surface, cross-linkers such as EDC/NHS mainly target antibodies. In fact, the reactions between crosslinkers and these functional groups are not selective, which leads to random orientation of antibodies [34]. To form amide bond between amine groups of antibodies and carboxyl groups of Cu@CDs, a solution containing EDC (as the most popular cross-linker for bioconjugations) (20 mM) and NHS (40 mM) was pipetted on the GCE/CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs-HQ to cover its surface [35]. In this step, EDC reacted with the carboxylic acid groups to form o-acylisourea. Then, the highly reactive chemical o-acylisourea reacted with NHS and formed an NHS ester [36]. After 1 h, 10 µM NH2-Ab solution was dropped on the electrode surface to react with the formed NHS ester at the room temperature for 45 min. The electrode was eluted three times with the PBS (pH=7.2) to release the unbound Abs from its surface. At last, to prevent non-specific adsorption of various proteins, a 0.25 % bovine serum albumin solution was dropped onto the modified electrode surface, allowed to stand for 15 min, and then washed three times with PBS [37]. BSA, as a reagent against non-specific adsorption, is widely used to block the spaces left on the solid surfaces (electrodes) after immobilization of the biomolecules. In fact, it prevents the protein adsorption except the target protein by non-specific binding on surfaces [38–40].
Fabrication procedure of CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs-HQ/Ab-based electrochemical sensing device for T4 detection was schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 .

For the electrochemical characterization of the electrode modification steps, cyclic voltammetry (CV) was applied using an Autolab PGSTAT302N Potentiostate (bandwidth of over 1 MHz) containing a three-electrode cell system (WE: GCE, RE: Ag/AgCl, CE: Pt). The CV voltammograms were determined in the PBS (0.1 M, pH=7.2) for different electrodes: GCE/CuO nanoparticles/HQ, GCE/Cu@CDs/HQ, GCE/CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs/HQ, GCE/CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs/HQ/Anti-T4 Ab, GCE/CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs/HQ/Anti-T4 Ab/T4.

To detect T4 using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) method, various amounts of T4 (0 to 8.0 nM) were pipetted on the surface of GCE/CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs/HQ/Ab and incubated for 40 min at room temperature. Then, the electrode was eluted with buffer to release unbound T4 species. Finally, the DPV voltammograms were recorded for each addition of T4 in the PBS (0.1 M, pH=7.2). The DPV parameters, such as pulse amplitude of 0 to 0.6 V, pulse amplitude of 0.1 V, scan rate of 0.1 V s-1 and pulse period of 0.01 s were selected as the optimal values ​​to achieve satisfactory sensor response.

Morphological characterization of CuO nanoparticles was performed using an EVO family modular scanning electron microscope (SEM) from ZEISS, Germany. SEM image of CuO nanoparticles demonstrated a morphology similar to nanoflowers (Fig. 2 A). However, the hierarchical structure of CuO assembled by a bunch of well-defined nanosheets is not clearly visible in this figure. In addition, the presence of copper and oxygen in the energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrum confirmed the formation of nanosized CuO (inset of Fig. 2A).

The crystallographic structure of CuO nanoparticles was studied by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Philips) (Fig. 2B). The XRD spectrum displayed two distinct peaks at 2θ = 35.555 and 39.005, corresponding to Miller indices (111) and (200) [41,42]. The existence of the two strong peaks according to the reference code ICSD 98–004–3179 further supported the pure phase of monoclinic CuO nanoparticles [42]. The Miller indices of all the peaks in XRD spectrum were represented by (111), (200), (202), (020), (20–2), (022), (202), (113), (311), (220) and (004) [42].
The XPS spectrum of Cu-doped CDs exhibited the presence of elements C1s, N1s, O1s, and Cu2p with binding energies of 284.5 eV, 399.6 eV, 530.5 eV and 936.0 eV, respectively (Fig. 3 A).

The C1s spectrum consisted of peaks at 284.1, 285.2, 287.8eV, which were well-fitted into C–C, CC and C–O/CO, respectively. Two peaks at 399.4 and 401.8 eV in the N1s spectrum can be ascribed to C–N and CN, respectively. The observed peaks at 530.6 and 532.1 eV in the O1s spectrum were attributed to CO/Cu–O and C–O–C, respectively [43].

As shown in the inset of Fig. 3A, the Cu2p spectrum composed of two peaks at 934.1 eV and 953.0 eV, which were corresponded to the spin orbit splitting of Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2, respectively [6,43–46]. Here, the Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 peaks are well separated. More importantly, the binding energy difference of about 19.0 eV between these two copper peaks is the same as the standard spectrum of copper. Despite the fact that chemical state determination using XPS analysis has become common for most elements of the periodic table, this method for the detection of copper oxidation state is challenging owing to the complexity of the Cu2p spectra resulting from the Cu2+-containing shake-up structures and overlapping binding energies for Cu0 metal and Cu+ species [46,47]. According to the previous reports on Cu@CD nanozyme [6,43–45], the 2p3/2 level was attributed to Cu+ or Cu0 and 2p1/2 level was ascribed to Cu+. Based on these findings, it can be estimated that the state of copper valence in laccase-like Cu@CDs is most likely between 0 and +1. To characterize the surface chemical composition of Cu@CDs.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM, EM10C, ZEISS, Germany) image of Cu@CDs (Fig. 3B) showed a semi-spherical morphology with a mean particle size of ∼8 nm. The Z-average particle size that represents the intensity-weighted mean hydrodynamic size/diameter (DH) of the total particles and particle size distribution were determined based on the dynamic light scattering (DLS) using Malvern Zetasizer ZS90 (UK). It was found to be ∼9 nm with polydispersity index (PDI) =0.32 (Inset of Fig. 2C). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, BesTec, Germany) was utilized to provide the surface elemental composition of Cu@CD, along with determining the oxidation state of copper. To ensure the stability of Cu-CDs, the size of particles stored at 4 °C (in the refrigerator) was determined three times, and the results showed that no significant increase in particle size was observed during 30 days.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of Cu@CDs was conducted using a mid-IR spectrometer (Vector 22, Brucker, Germany). According to Fig. 3C, a broad peak belonging to the stretching vibrations of O–H or N–H functional groups was appeared at 3429.3 cm-1. An absorption peak at 2930.7 cm−1 was attributed to the C–H stretching vibration, while the distinguishing bands at 1651.9 cm−1 and 1384.8 cm−1 were ascribed to the C=O and C–N stretching vibrations. Moreover, the characteristic absorption peaks at 1093.6 cm−1 and 1534.3 cm−1 were matched with stretching vibrations of C–O and C=C. The results obtained from the FTIR spectrum of Cu@CDs are consistent with the FTIR pattern of previous research conducted by Guo and co-workers [45]. The appearance of high-frequency band at 600.1 cm-1 can be related to the stretching vibration of Cu-O [46].

The as-produced Cu-doped CDs showed a one-photon long-wavelength fluorescence (FL) emission at 520.4 nm (taken on Perkin Elmer LS 55 fluorescence spectrometer, USA) (Fig. 3D, curve a). Cu-doped CDs showed a strong enhancement of fluorescence intensity compared to the undoped Persian willow-derived carbon dots (Fig. 3D, curve b). In fact, the fluorescence intensity was enhanced by copper doping. Currently, elemental doping is the most common method to increase the fluorescence performance of CDs [48–50]. Since metals act as electron donors and have a larger atomic radius, they can cause changes in charge density and charge transfer forms between metal ions and carbon matrix, which ultimately lead to further improvement of the fluorescence intensity of CDs doped with metals compared with CDs doped with heteroatoms [51–53] The synthesized Cu@CDs displayed uniformly dispersed size, excellent fluorescence properties and good water solubility. In addition, the Cu-CD solution emitted obvious blue fluorescence under UV lamp [6] (inset of Fig. 3D).

Step-by-step modification of electrode along with determination mechanism of immunosensor was characterized using by CV. Since the most widely-used method to monitor the properties of electrocatalysts is voltammetry [54], it was selected to show the electrocatalytic oxidation of substrate.

As observed in Fig. 4 A, the current intensity of GCE/CuO nanoparticles/HQ (curve a) was considerably lower than the signal of GCE/Cu@CDs/HQ (curve b). The reason for that was speed up the transfer of electrons between electrode and HQ signal probe, which led to an increase in current. Since the oxidation of HQ to 1,4-benzoquinone (para-quinone) has been reported to be very slow (long-term reaction) in the catalyst absence at room temperature, the introduction of Cu-containing nanozymes to the electrode surface results in an acceleration of the oxidation reaction up to 57 times compared to the free-catalyst oxidation reaction [55]. Interestingly, enhanced electrocatalytic HQ oxidation on GCE/Cu@CDs occurred at a significantly decreased potential. It is interpreted that electrocatalysts can facilitate electrochemical reactions by lowering the activation energies required for the reactions [56]. The increased current and the reduced potential of the GCE/Cu@CDs/HQ (curve b) compared to the GCE/CuO/HQ (curve a) confirm the enzyme-like activity of Cu@CDs. As the electrode was modified by CuO/Cu@CDs (curve c), the current intensity clearly increased compared to the GCE/Cu@CDs, due to high conductivity of CuO nanoparticles that can improve the electron transfer efficiency. On the other hand, the high electrical conductivity as well as large specific surface area of GCE/CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs/HQ led to a remarkable increase in the current of curve c compared to curve b. The CuO nanostructures exhibit good electrical conductivity and very low internal resistance [42,57], so they can be used in the electrochemically stable supercapacitors.

By exposing the electrode to the antibody solution (10 μM, pH=7.2), the antibodies were attached to the carbon dots and subsequently covered the active sites of the Cu@CDs nanozyme. As a result, the electron transfer induced by the oxidation of HQ signal probe was blocked [58]. Curve d exhibited the effect of immobilizing antibodies to the GCE/CuO/Cu@CDs/HQ on the covering of the active sites of the nanozyme. Similar to the previous studies [58,59], laccase-mimicking Cu@CDs activity was affected by masking of their enzymatic active sites. The introduction of T4 to the modified electrode surface and its subsequent binding with anti-T4 Ab further covered the active sites of the nanozyme, leading to further blocking of electron transfer. Based on this mechanism, the peak current of GCE/CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs-HQ/Ab further decreased with the addition of T4 antigen (40 pM) to the modified electrode surface (curve e).

The above results show that the electron transfer can be controlled by changing the surface composition of electrode. In addition, the linear behavior of peak current with the scan rate indicates that the electrochemical process is surface controlled (also known as an adsorption-controlled). The peak currents for the HQ oxidation at the GCE/CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs/HQ increased linearly when the scan rate increased from 10 to 100 mV s −1.
Fig. 4B shows the masking of the active sites of the nanozyme by antibodies that leads to the blocking of electron transfer between HQ and Cu@CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing concentration of signal and capture probes
ce:section.ce:para: Concentration of the signal tag is known as a key parameter for label-free electrochemical biosensors [60]. Label-free means biosensing without covalent labelling of any capturing biomolecule [60]. Here, we report a deep investigation into the effect of the concentration of HQ as a redox probe on electrode current. For this purpose, different concentrations of HQ were pipetted on the GCE/CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs and then the electrode signal was recorded. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the current signal of electrode demonstrated an increasing trend with the increase of HQ concentration up to 0.05 mM, and then it stabilized with the saturation of the electrode surface. As expected, this trend is according to the previous articles [58,61].

Since bioreceptors are considered as the key components for electrochemical biosensing in the biological applications, their density (concentration) can affect the sensor signal [58,61–63]. In this experiment, GCE/CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs-HQ was exposed to the solution of antibody with different concentrations from 2 µM to 14 µM and then electrode current was recorded. Acoording to Fig. 5B, the current intensity of GCE/CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs-HQ decreased with increasing anti-T4 antibody concentration and reached a minimum at the concentration of 10 μM. The steric repulsion of fixed antibodies and saturation of modified electrode surface prevented further reduction of the electrode current [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimal pH for binding antibody to thyroxine
ce:section.ce:para: The number of binding sites (n) on the T4-specific Ab remains constant over the pH range 4–9. Below pH 4 and above pH 9, n decreases [64]. On the other hand, association constant (Kass) between T4 and the Ab is best between pH 4 and 5. In fact, the antibody binds L-thyroxine maximally between pH 4 and 5 with an association constant of ca. 1 × 1010 M−1. Although, Kass changes with pH in the range 4–9, the magnitude of this change is not large compared to binding at pH 3 or 10 [64]. Therefore, considering the Kass and binding sites on the antibody, physiological pH (pH=7.2) is chosen for the interaction of the T4-specific Ab with T4. At the physiological pH (pH=7.2), the binding sites on the antibody reach their maximum value, while the Kass does not differ significantly from its maximum value at pH 4 to 5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of incubation time for antibody-thyroxine interaction
ce:section.ce:para: The incubation time of antibody-target interaction can play a critical role in the reliable and accurate disease diagnosis and prediction of biosensor outcome [65]. The previous results on antibody-antigen (Ab-Ag) binding time at room temperature confirmed 30 min to 2 h as the optimal incubation time for maximum response [66]. According to these data, we examined the immunosensor response at different Ab-Ag interaction times from 30 min to 1 h. The results showed that the electrode current reaches a maximum in 45 mins and then becomes stable due to the completion of the reaction (Fig. 5C).

The analytical response of the GCE/CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs-HQ/Ab at different concentrations of T4 was investigated using DPV in the PBS (0.1 M, pH=7.2), as seen in Fig. 6 A. It was observed that the peak current diminished from 37.9 µA to 12.8 µA as the T4 concentration enhanced from 0.0 to 8.0 nM. This can be ascribed to the more coverage of the active sites of nanozyme at higher levels of T4, which leads to a significant decrease in electron transfer. The nanozyme-enabled immunosensor was calibrated at 0.18 V and showed a linear relation between the current intensity as the dependent variable and T4 concentration as the independent variable. The electrode response changes in this potential (0.18 V) were linear with variations in T4 biomarker concentration in the range of 10.0 pM-8.0 nM according to the equation I (µA) = - 7.304 log (CT4) (pM) + 41.379 (R2=0.9872) (Fig. 6B). In fact, binding of the target resulted in the decreased electron transfer efficiency, enabling signal-off-based T4 determination. The LOD (limit of detection) was estimated based on the 3σ/m rule, and was found to be 2.8 pM. In fact, calibration plot consisted of seven points was created from the instrumental response to a set of standard samples at a DLR (dynamic linear range) of 10.0 pM-8.0 nM. Moreover, the test was further validated using clinical T4 samples from human serum. The samples were measured in the concentration range from 10.0 pM to 8.0 nM. Expressed by equation equation I (µA) = - 6.577 log (CT4) (pM) + 40.058 (R2=0.9331) was observed (Fig. 6C). The R2 values of the clinical T4 samples were lower compared with those of the T4 standards. The LOD was found to be 3.4 pM.

The obtained LOD and DLR were compared to few previously reported studies, as presented in Table 1 [67–73]. The LOD of the present signal-off immunosensor was found to be lower than the LOD of other methods. The engineered Cu@CDs-dependent signaling pathway provided wide DLR. More importantly, it could cover the normal range of free thyroxine present in the human serum samples (11.5–22.7 pM) [74].

Selectivity of GCE/CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs-HQ/Ab was evaluated at the presence of interferes such as TSH (1 nM), BSA (1 nM), PTH (1 nM), hemoglobin (1 nM) and their mixture in the PBS (0.1 M, pH=7.2). As seen in Fig. 6D, the impact of interfering proteins on the determination of T4 protein (400 pM) were not found to be considerable. So, the described electrochemical immunosensor was able to detect T4 biomarker with high selectivity.

The repeatability of electrochemical immunosensor was evaluated by repeating the measurement of a fixed expectation level of T4 (5 nM) in the PBS (0.1 M, pH=7.2) for 5 times while the conditions were kept constant [58,75]. The obtained findings showed that the immunosensor responses have no remarkable changes in 5 determination repetitions (RSD = 1.6 %).

One of the critical limitations of biosensors is the lack of stability over time [76]. Poor stability of immunesensors is a big challenge hindering their electrochemical efficiency. Therefore, this factor should be considered in the design of biosensors. In this work, the long-term stability of GCE/CuO nanoparticles/Cu@CDs-HQ/Ab was investigated after its storage in the PBS at 4 °C (in the refrigerator) for 30 days [62]. After 10 days, the immunosensor signal was 99.3 % of the initial response. While after 20 and 30 days, the immunosensor signals reached 98.7 % and 98.1 % of the initial responses, respectively (Fig. 6E).
The electroactive surface area for GCE and GCE/CuO/Cu@CDs was determined from the cyclic voltammograms in solution of 5 mM of K3[Fe(CN)6] containing 0.1 M KCl at various sweep rates.

where Ip (A) is the anodic peak current, n is the number of electron transfers, A (cm2) is the surface area of the electrode, C (mol cm−3) is the concentration of K3[Fe(CN)6], D (cm2 s−1) is the diffusion coefficient and υ (V s−1) is the scan rate.

From the slope of the ip vs. υ1/2 line, the values of A were determined as 0.067 cm2 and 0.196 cm2 for the bare GCE and GCE/CuO/Cu@CDs, respectively. Therefore, after the electrode surface was modified with CuO/Cu@CDs, the electroactive surface area greatly increased. As the particle size decreases, its total surface area per unit volume increases. Therefore, nanostructures, especially quantum dots, significantly increase the electrode surface area. The available active surface area and the density of probes immobilized on the modified electrode surface are responsible for achieving high sensitivity in electrochemical biosensors [78].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of T4 in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: An important drawback of the biosensor interface is biofouling (the undesirable formation of proteins on a surface immersed in biological fluids). On the other hand, when the biosensors are immerged in the urine, serum or blood, proteins deposit on the surface of the interface layer. These proteins (the deposited layer) limit the conformational dynamics of bioreceptors connected to the electrode and subsequently the electron transfer efficiency between the redox reporter and electrode [76]. So, the analytical performance and accuracy of biosensors in the clinical samples is essential for commercializing these systems [79]. In this study, the feasibility of designed electrochemical immunosensor was evaluated using spike-recovery (add/found) test. Spike-recovery method by adding known amounts of analyte into a certain amount of sample is one of the acceptable approaches for accuracy validation of analytical results. It is especially used to evaluate the analytical method for biological and pharmaceutical samples because the conditions of native analytes are the same as spiked analytes in pharmaceutical and biological samples [80]. In this study, three serum samples collected from healthy volunteers (normal range: 11.5–22.7 pM) were first diluted 5-fold with t-Octylphenoxypolyethoxyethanol non-ionic surfactant/detergent (0.1 % v/v) to reduce the interference of the serum matrix [58,62,81]. Then, known amounts of T4 (10 pM, 400 pM and 7000 pM) are added to the diluted serum samples and analyzed four times. Finally, the recoveries of different concentrations of spiked thyroxine were evaluated in the serum samples (Table 2 ). The obtained recoveries showed a high accuracy for the analysis of serum samples (≥95 %). These results indicate that the designed immunosensor has a high potential for the determination of serum T4 level in the patients with hyperthyroidism or hypothyroidism.

Since the T4 test helps to diagnose thyroid diseases, we decided to design an electrochemical immunosensor to determine this biomarker. This sensing device was designed based on the electrocatalysis of HQ oxidation using the laccase-mimicking Cu@CDs. The modification of electrode with the Cu-containing nanozymes accelerates the oxidation reaction of HQ up to 57 times compared to the same reaction without an electrocatalyst. The modification of electrode surface with CuO nanostructures led to an increase in the current intensity owing to the high conductivity and large specific surface area of nanoparticles. However, the activity of Cu@CDs was affected by the addition of antibodies to the electrode surface, which were able to mask the active sites of the nanozyme and subsequently reduce the immunosensor response. Introducing T4 to the modified electrode surface masked the active sites of the Cu@CDs more and led to further blocking of the electron transfer. So, the current intensity diminished from 37.9 µA to 12.8 µA as the T4 level enhanced from 0.0 to 8.0 nM. The response changes were linear with variations in T4 biomarker concentration in the range of 10.0 pM-8.0 nM. The detection limit was found to be 2.8 pM, which was lower compared to the LODs reported in previous studies. In addition, spike recovery studies demonstrated the high capability of the designed immunosensor to evaluate serum T4 levels in patients with hyperthyroidism or hypothyroidism. The present immunosensor has advantages such as high selectivity, wide linear range, good repeatability and excellent long-term stability. Despite all these outstanding merits, the T4 determination mechanism by the current immunosensor is based on a signal-off strategy, which has more noise compared to signal strategies [58,82].
